Oil Prices Slide as Funds Ready for Gulf Exports to Resume

Investors have halved their position in Brent anticipating a pickup exports through the Strait of Hormuz as talks between the United States and Iran intensify and more vessels proceed through the waterway under cover of darkness.

Hedge funds and other money managers sold the equivalent of another 44 million barrels of futures and options in the ICE Brent contract over the seven days ending on June 9, according to position records filed with the exchange ...

quotedTweet.text:
Take this from the guy that told you Cushing will tank bottom at the end of June. 

Cushing’s relevance to global oil prices wasn’t what it was like before. 

US has access to landlocked crude from Canada that goes directly to PADD 2. Couple that with 13.7 million b/d of US crude oil production and Cushing is really just a storage hub shifting barrels from one point to the next. 

However, the fact that the market is still pricing US barrels as if we have an abundance is wrong. We are reaching operational minimums, and the market should price the barrels out. 

But it is what it is.

However, the bank warned prices could exceed $110/bbl if disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz persist.

Daily Market Report #764 TLDR

It is Saturday. Full World Cup fever here.

I am tipping most games on prediction markets and I am 2 out of 4 so far.

The $SPCX IPO happened yesterday, and it is all the market wants to talk about.

Trading around $160 today.

Pre-IPO perps had it at $180.

US equities added $1.15T in a day, Nasdaq up 3%.

Gold is still soft at $4,180, and the war premium leaving gold and oil tells you the market is de-risking the geopolitical trade.

$BTC is sitting at $63,487, basically flat.

Dominance 56.4%.

Spot BTC ETFs logged a fifth straight outflow day and have bled $2.1B since June started.

The institutional bid is still missing, but Morgan Stanley raised its Bitcoin holdings to 3,850 BTC via spot ETF.

$ETH is at $1,664, flat.

ETH TVL is up again, and 7-day flows are finally green across Ethereum, Solana, Arbitrum, and Base for the first time in over a month.

$SOL is at $66.73, flat, but it had the strongest fundamental week in my universe.

SPCX trading rails.

WSOP sponsorship.

Superteam Germany hosting the largest Solana event ever in Berlin.

Token doing nothing.

Ecosystem shipping hard.

$XRP is at $1.13.

JPMorgan and Ripple are testing 5-second XRP settlement, the kind of real-bank pilot XRP holders waited years for.

$HYPE is at $58.16.

Circle transferred $4B $USDC to Hyperliquid, an enormous liquidity vote even as the token cools.

$XMR is at $344, cooling after a huge week but still the privacy leader.

$ZEC is at $414.

AI is now both the auditor and the attacker, and after the Opus 4.8 audit found the Zcash bug, the security layer clearly has not caught up.

The collector itch is real.

Avalanche Treasury sank 38% on its Nasdaq debut, a reminder that the crypto-treasury-goes-public trade is not a guaranteed win.

NFTfi is shutting down as NFT lending contracts.

Japan passed a crypto bill classifying assets as financial instruments and cutting taxes, a real regulated-market unlock. $ADA is done. 

Bezos’s AI venture Prometheus raised $12B.

ChatGPT hit 1B monthly app users.

And developers are now saying Claude Fable 5 plays it too safe.

After I praised it earlier this week for fixing things I had not thought of, the broader dev consensus is that the safety guardrails went too far.

What are you watching this weekend?

quotedTweet.text:
Daily Market Report #764

It is Saturday. Full World Cup fever here.

I am tipping most of the games on prediction markets, and I am 2 out of 4 so far. Not a disaster, not a brag. Football is humbling in the same way trading is. You can read everything right and still watch a red card in the 12th minute torch your whole thesis. I will keep sharing the slips, wins and losses both.

The SpaceX IPO happened yesterday, and it is the only thing the market wants to talk about. Priced at $135, Elon is officially a trillionaire, and the stock is trading around $160 today after the pre-IPO perps had it at $180.

The detail I want to flag: Solana was the only platform where you could trade SPCX out of the gate yesterday. Not Hyperliquid, not a TradFi broker, Solana. On a day everyone was focused on Hyperliquid’s pre-IPO perps, Solana quietly had the rails working when it counted.

Stocks already ripped, US equities added $1.15T in a day, Nasdaq up 3%.

Gold $4,180, still soft. The war premium leaving gold and oil at the same time tells you this is real de-risking of the geopolitical trade, not fear.

Bitcoin $63,487, basically flat. Dominance 56.4%. Holding the range while everything around it moves. Spot BTC ETFs logged a fifth straight outflow day and have bled $2.1B since June started, with ETF volumes down 78% from peak.

The institutional bid is still missing.

But Morgan Stanley raised its Bitcoin holdings to 3,850 BTC via spot ETF, so it is not everyone leaving. It is rotation and thinning volume.

Saylor repeated his line again: never said Strategy would not sell.

He is going to keep saying it until people stop being angry, which will be never.

Metaplanet bought a Japanese securities firm to build BTC-linked products, the treasury-company-becomes-a-bank playbook continuing.

Ethereum $1,664, flat. ETH TVL up another 0.9%, and 7-day flows are finally green across Ethereum, Solana, Arbitrum, and Base for the first time in over a month.

The bleed has genuinely stopped at the chain level, even if price is flat.

Vitalik teased liquidation-free DeFi on the way, which would be a real structural shift if it ships.

Solana $66.73, flat, but the strongest story in my universe this week on fundamentals.

The SPCX trading rails, the WSOP sponsorship, and Superteam Germany hosting the largest Solana event ever in Berlin.

While the token does nothing, the Solana ecosystem is throwing the biggest parties and shipping the most product.

That gap usually resolves up eventually.

XRP $1.13, down 1%.

JPMorgan and Ripple are testing 5-second XRP settlement, which is the kind of real-bank pilot XRP holders have waited years for.

Sentiment at multi-month lows, usage down 91%, and the banks are quietly testing it anyway.

Hyperliquid $58.16, flat. Circle transferred $4B USDC to Hyperliquid, an enormous liquidity vote even as the token cools.

The Kalshi-perps competition story from yesterday still stands, but $4B in fresh stablecoin liquidity says the platform is not going anywhere.

Beaten token, thriving venue.

Monero $344, down 6.5%. Giving back some of the 18% week, but still the privacy leader.

Zcash $414, down 4.5%.

The privacy bid cools on a flat day, which is normal after a rip.

Immunefi’s CEO called AI the biggest new threat to DeFi smart contract security, which lands hard given the Opus 4.8 audit that found the Zcash bug.

AI is now both the auditor and the attacker, and the security layer has not caught up.

ARB unlock Tuesday and STRK unlock Monday, both worth watching into thin weekend liquidity.

Pengu $0.0067, and the Pudgy Vibes TCG Season 3 window is still open.

Avalanche Treasury sank 38% on its Nasdaq debut, a rough reminder that the crypto-treasury-goes-public trade is not a guaranteed win.

NFTfi is shutting down as the NFT lending market contracts, and that one stings as a collector.

The quiet infrastructure of the last NFT cycle is closing one door at a time.

Japan passed a crypto bill classifying assets as financial instruments and cutting taxes, a genuinely big regulated-market unlock.

SpaceX aside, the numbers stay surreal.

Bezos’s AI venture Prometheus raised $12B.

ChatGPT hit 1B monthly app users.

And the line that matters most: developers are now saying Claude Fable 5 plays it too safe.

After I praised it earlier this week for fixing things I had not thought of, the broader dev consensus is that the safety guardrails went too far.

Anthropic walked back the sabotage policy, and the market is telling them the model is too cautious. It’s crazy also that US stoped Fable.

Fascinating problem to have, building something so capable that the debate is whether it is too careful.

What are you watching this weekend?

text: One shock hit #India's export side. The other hit the import side. #US tariffs at 50% and Brent crude at US$120 tested the domestic-demand growth model simultaneously, and the classical adjustment mechanisms worked only partially: @BardhanArya https://t.co/KqUQXm30F0

Trump, who promised "no new wars," is begging for an exit, and Iran knows it.

Inflation hit 4.2% in May. Energy prices jumped 23.5%. 97,000 jobs cut last month.

Iran controls the Strait of Hormuz now. 20% percent of global oil flows through that waterway. Iran shut it, reopened it, and shut it again. The US cannot fix this. 

Iran now has a "toll booth" in the strait that did not exist before the war, and it is likely permanent.

Tump's approval slumped to 42.5%. 
70% disapprove of his economy. Betting markets give him a 12% impeachment chance by year end.

Trump is now claiming a deal is close. But Iran has not made a final decision. Why would they? They hold the strait. They hold the leverage. They hold the cards.

Everyone. Copy the post. Paste into grok. Read the real story. And here it is:
This framing mixes accurate quotes with misleading spin. Here's the factual picture based on current 2026 developments.
Strait of Hormuz
Trump's      statement: Yes, President Trump has repeatedly said the Strait of Hormuz      would be "open to all" immediately after a deal is signed (e.g.,      Truth Social posts and public remarks in June 2026). The proposed      memorandum of understanding (MOU) includes Iran clearing mines it      deployed, no tolls, and free passage during a 60-day ceasefire extension.
https://t.co/gAGSrgegNU
Pre-war      status: It was open to commercial shipping before the Feb. 28, 2026,      U.S.-Israel strikes on Iranian targets (nuclear sites, military assets).      Iran then effectively closed/blocked it in retaliation by mining,      threatening vessels, and restricting traffic — slashing global oil flows      (the strait normally handles ~20-25% of seaborne oil). The U.S. responded      with a blockade of Iranian ports.
https://t.co/QCzIOGcryf
Reopening      it is a core goal to ease global energy prices, not a concession to the      pre-strike status quo alone.
Blocked Iranian funds (~$24B)
Iranian      state media (Mehr, Fars) and officials claim the draft deal includes      releasing $24 billion in frozen/overseas assets during the 60-day      negotiation window (half potentially upfront).
https://t.co/RQ0WudJUDp
Trump      and U.S. officials have pushed back: Trump stated "no money will      change hands" in some comments, with releases tied to compliance      (e.g., nuclear steps, no cash for weapons). Any unfreezing would likely      involve humanitarian channels, tranches, or waivers rather than direct      payments. This remains a sticking point in negotiations mediated by      Pakistan.
https://t.co/RKr6JifrbF
Relation to JCPOA
Not a      return to the Obama-era JCPOA: Trump withdrew from the 2015 Joint      Comprehensive Plan of Action in 2018, citing its flaws (sunset clauses, no      limits on missiles/proxies, insufficient inspections). The current effort      is a new, temporary MOU/ceasefire framework focused on:
Ending       active hostilities (including Lebanon).
Reopening       the strait.
Initial       nuclear curbs (e.g., enriched uranium).
Broader       talks on Iran's program.
It is      not re-entering the original deal. Trump has described it as tougher      ("wall to no nuclear weapon"). Pre-withdrawal, Iran was      compliant with core limits; post-withdrawal and especially post-2026 war,      Iran's program advanced significantly.
https://t.co/u3WgjORtN7
The 2026 conflict (strikes, Iranian retaliation, blockade) created the current disruptions. The emerging deal aims to de-escalate while addressing U.S. red lines on nukes and shipping freedom — standard great-power diplomacy, not "back to before." Details are still fluid as of mid-June 2026, with both sides spinning progress and timelines. Outcomes depend on implementation, not rhetoric.

🚨 IRAN WAR: DAY 107 UPDATE 🇮🇷🇺🇸

The biggest question today:

📜 Will a deal actually be signed?

Conflicting messages continue to emerge from Washington, Tehran, and mediators.

📌 WHERE THINGS STAND

🇺🇸 Trump says a deal is scheduled to be signed today.

🇵🇰 Pakistan has indicated an electronic signing could take place.

🇮🇷 Iran's Foreign Ministry says no agreement will be signed today and insists negotiations are not yet finalized.

⚠️ MIXED SIGNALS

As has been the case throughout the conflict:

➡️ Claims

➡️ Counterclaims

➡️ Conflicting timelines

continue to dominate headlines.

Iranian media reported proposals that included massive reconstruction demands, reportedly worth up to $300 billion.

Trump responded by saying those terms have:

❌ "NOTHING to do with the terms that were agreed to."

⚔️ MILITARY ACTIVITY CONTINUES

🚁 The U.S. says it intercepted drones heading toward the Strait of Hormuz overnight.

🚢 A tanker was reportedly struck by an unknown projectile off the coast of Oman.

🛢️ Shipping and energy markets remain on alert.

🧠 WHY IT MATTERS

Markets are trying to determine whether:

✅ A genuine breakthrough is imminent

or

❌ Negotiations remain far from complete.

The outcome could have major implications for:

🛢️ Oil prices

🚢 Global shipping

📈 Equity markets

🌍 Middle East stability

💭 BOTTOM LINE

Diplomatic momentum appears stronger than at any point in the last 107 days.

But until Washington and Tehran publicly confirm the same terms and the same timeline, uncertainty remains high.

The next 24–48 hours could determine whether this conflict moves toward a lasting agreement—or another round of escalation.

India was supposed to run out of Fuel and Gas in 25 days.

Yet after 100 days of war, everything is normal in the country.

When the Iran war erupted, experts predicted India would run out of fuel in weeks.

The logic seemed simple.

India imports over 85% of its crude oil.

The Strait of Hormuz carries nearly 20% of global oil trade.

Block Hormuz and India suffers.

Yet months later, there are no fuel queues, no rationing, no shortages.

Why?

Because India was never sitting on just "25 days of reserves."

Officially, India had around 60 days of actual stock cover and 74 days of total reserve capacity. More importantly, India had already secured alternative supplies before panic reached television studios.

The real story is bigger than oil.

It is about how Modi transformed India from an energy buyer into an energy power.

When the crisis came, India did not ask who would save it.

India had already prepared to save itself.

That is the difference between governance and headlines.

The most underrated Modi-era achievement is diversification.

A decade ago, India was heavily dependent on a handful of suppliers and vulnerable maritime routes.

Today India imports crude from around 40 countries.

Before the crisis, about 55% of imports came through routes outside Hormuz.

During the war, that number jumped to 70%.

Think about the scale of that shift.

While analysts obsessed over one chokepoint, India quietly built alternatives across Russia, the US, Latin America, Africa and Central Asia.

This is the difference between dependence and resilience.

A nation dependent on one supplier negotiates from weakness.

A nation with 40 suppliers negotiates from strength.

Modi's energy strategy ensured that if one door closed, ten others remained open.

The result?

Global panic.

Indian fuel pumps operating normally.

The biggest reason India survived was not just reserves.

It was diplomacy.

For years critics mocked Modi's approach of maintaining strong ties with the United States, Russia, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Israel and Europe simultaneously.

The Iran war proved why that strategy mattered.

Russia reportedly stood ready to divert 9.5 million barrels of crude toward India and indicated it could support up to 40% of India's crude requirements if needed.

Gulf nations continued honoring long-term commitments.

Western countries understood India's energy compulsions.

This is what strategic autonomy looks like.

India buys Russian oil without becoming anti-West.

India deepens ties with Israel without losing the Gulf.

India partners America without becoming dependent on America.

Most countries choose camps.

India chose leverage.

That is "Mo"diplomacy.

And leverage is the most valuable currency in geopolitics.

The crisis also exposed how much infrastructure India has built over the last decade.

India's strategic petroleum reserve capacity stands at 5.33 MMT, with another 6.5 MMT approved.

Refineries capable of processing over 256 million metric tonnes annually were operating at more than 100% utilization.

LPG production was increased by 40%, raising domestic output to over 60% of national demand.

The number of LPG import terminals doubled from 11 in 2014 to 22 today.

Most citizens never noticed these numbers.

But they are the reason life remained normal.

Strong nations are not measured by how they perform during normal times.

They are measured by how they perform during shocks.

The Iran war became India's stress test.

The system passed.

The deeper lesson is not about oil.

It is about India's rise.

In previous decades, a crisis in West Asia would trigger fears of shortages, inflation and economic instability.

Today India can absorb global shocks because it spent years building reserves, infrastructure, supply chains and diplomatic influence.

The Iran war demonstrated something profound.

The world's third-largest energy consumer is no longer a passive participant in global energy markets.

THE SECOND HALF OF 2026 and so on..
OIL, AI, REFLATION, UNDERPRICED CONVEXITY

I have been looking at IV, rho, and funding rates on long-dated $SPX options. (As a reminder, rho measures the sensitivity of the option premium to interest rates).
The steepness of the term structure is surprisingly calm, no kink, albeit elevated. It is no coincidence that I mentioned last year that I'm long ATM straddles. However, there is a significant increase and asymmetry in option premiums, which stems purely from the carry. The funding rate is well above the dividend yield (SOFR 3.60%, Fed Funds 3.50-3.75%).
For the expiries between Dec 17 and Jan 14, I also calculated that the annualized forward drift over this 28-day window comes out to exactly ~6.06%. This is not driven by rate hike fears, but is rather a synthetic usurious rate charged by banks.
The market is explicitly pricing in a slow hiking cycle, one that—both in velocity and magnitude—allows real interest rates to erode further, acting as a hidden QE effect for equities. The market hopes that under political and fiscal constraints, Warsh will be unable to initiate a steeper rate path and will instead, much like Trump, prioritize managing the debt burden (as I pointed out last year). The market sees that the 4.12% bond yield is being eaten alive by reflation, so participants are willing to pay the banks' 4.75% toll just to buy the SPX at a 3.73% earnings yield. This is a painful, reflation-driven melt-up scenario.

☝️What they are failing to account for, however, is that an energy shock by autumn is no longer a mere possibility, but a reality.
The SPR is at 350M barrels, hovering at 1983 lows. Furthermore, the US government is a forced buyer in the future due to the exchange program. The first solicitation is for 86M barrels with an 18–22% in-kind premium, with returns scheduled between Nov 2026 and Sept 2028; the replenishment will bring levels roughly back to the current baseline around July 2028. The reason oil pulled back from $144 to $110, and why inflation hasn't struck yet, is the record-breaking coordinated inventory release. The IEA released 400M+ barrels (301M of which was crude), providing a ~2.5 mb/d buffer for four months—but this is time-limited, as the fixed release volume will rapidly deplete. The weekly SPR drawdown was 9.92M barrels (week of May 15), breaking the 2022 record, and total US crude inventories plunged by ~17.8M barrels in a single week—the largest drop since 1982. Meanwhile, the SPR is approaching its ~240M operational minimum, with a ~70M functional floor.

The market is therefore pricing this as if the shock has passed (because oil pulled back and the release calmed things down), when in reality it is merely buffered, and that buffer will run dry around mid-summer, as I keep saying.
The second wave—price re-acceleration and forced replenishment demand (the government acting as a forced buyer at a +18–22% premium), compounded by the inflationary pass-through—is mechanically hardcoded. It is not hypothetical, and it is completely independent of whether Iran re-escalates or not.
Furthermore, if OECD reserves fall to their lowest levels since 2003 (50 days of forward cover), an exponential rise in energy prices becomes inevitable, forcing an aggressive rate hike. The AI sector is already overleveraged and overbuilt, as I and many others have demonstrated. However, this shock will force a reality check, which they are currently trying to suppress through headline and narrative control.

- Why is that?
Because the administration is doing everything in its power for the midterms. Every data point is framed to argue against a rate hike. The market is pricing in absolutely zero shock or tail risk. Although the term structure is elevated, it remains within an average range, and the skew is just business as usual.
The market, as I mentioned, is betting on a reflationary melt-up.

The trade is obvious.
First, I already stated last year that I am long long-dated ATM straddles. This has been generating a nice amount of income ever since....
Due to high forward rates, calls are already very expensive, but puts remain cheap, making the underpricing of downside convexity an excellent opportunity. Short 2027-2028 far-OTM calls (or spread) and use the premium to buy the cheap catalyst window that the volatility surface is mispricing (Dec '26 – Jun '27 put spreads).
🙂The risk here is if the shock hits slower, gets delayed, or gets drawn out. In that case, the put spread can be bled dry by theta, and the broker's risk algorithm could ruthlessly liquidate you via the margin requirements on the long-dated short calls before your macro thesis plays out.

Another option is a box spread arbitrage targeting the December-January usurious rate. The risk-free harvesting of this bank balance-sheet premium cleanly covers the theta bleed of my direction-neutral volatility positions.
It is important to continually reiterate that the SPX/yields beta is expected to remain negative, meaning bonds cannot be used as a hedge. This could change, and it is crucial to monitor, but the probability is low. Warsh will be a turning point: he will either confirm or reverse this trend.
Alongside all this, I am long the #oil curve. Due to backwardation, rolling down the curve allows me to continuously pocket a positive roll yield. This leg not only provides protection against the rate-shock scenario, but it constantly pays me to wait, massively improving the survivability of the entire portfolio.
(Remember how I was short backwardation when Iran conflict started? I signaled it here real time, broke it down for you. That trade also printed nicely... now this)
Enjoy.

As I keep saying: 
"Warsh is not priced in,
oil is not priced in,
SPR risk is not priced in,
energy crisis and credit risk is not priced in,
China First Island Chain is not priced in at all.
BoJ is only slightly priced in, market is too complacent about it."
I covered it all in the past months, you just don't listen. I forecasted things ahead of many. Listen to me more carefully... big opportunities there are...

US product stocks started 2026 above every prior year. 

⚠️They just fell below every prior year. 

The yellow line is 2026.

Weeks 1-5: highest stocks in years. 
US refiners well-supplied, cushion above historical norms.

Week 10: Iran war. Hormuz closes. US refiners ramp to maximum utilization to fill global gasoline, diesel and jet fuel gaps left by Middle East outages.

Week 21: stocks crash below every single prior year on the chart.

The cushion that absorbed the first months of the Hormuz shock is now gone.

What's left? refiners already running flat-out, product stocks below historical lows, and hurricane season just starting.

Any further disruption  a Gulf Coast storm, an unscheduled refinery outage, a demand spike hits a system with no inventory buffer and no spare capacity to respond.

This is what "the US is the swing refiner of last resort" looks like when the buffer runs out.

Gasoline and diesel cracks this summer are one bad headline away from spiking hard.

The world in June 2026.
Nothing is what it seems.

FIFA World Cup — happening in America.
Right now. Today.
The most watched event in human history.
5 billion people distracted.
Convenient timing.

While the world watches football —
Iran war ceasefire — announced April 7.
Still not ceased. June 14.
Someone benefits from it continuing.
It’s never the people dying.
UAE withdrew from OPEC — May 1.
Quietly. No headlines.
The oil cartel that controlled
the world’s energy for 50 years —
fracturing.
Nobody is talking about this.
Ebola — Congo. May 2026.
USAID defunded.
Response capacity — gone.
Outbreak spreading.
5 billion people watching Messi.

Trump turned 80 today.
Hosted UFC at the White House.
A billionaire cage fight
on the lawn of the most powerful building
in the world.
On his birthday.
While 3 Indian sailors are buried from US Navy strikes.
The optics —
if you’re paying attention —
are extraordinary.

Elon became a trillionaire.
Same week —
DOGE cut programs feeding the poorest people.
Wealth up $200 billion.
USAID — defunded.
The transfer of resources —
from the bottom to the top —
has never been faster.
Has never been more visible.
Has never been less discussed.

Ukraine uses AI drones now.
Ground robots. Autonomous.
Killing machines that don’t need orders.
The same AI companies —
OpenAI. Google DeepMind. Anthropic —
that say they’re building AI for humanity —
whose technology is in those drones?
Nobody is asking.

The conspiracy isn’t a theory.
It’s the oldest pattern in history —
distract the population.
Start a war.
Move the money.
Control the narrative.
The tools change.
The playbook doesn’t.
Football in 1936 — Berlin Olympics.
Football in 2026 — World Cup.
Both times —
the world was burning.
Both times —the crowd was cheering.

Pay attention to what’s happening
while you’re watching the game.
The most important moves are always made when the stadium is loudest.
#WorldNews #FIFA2026 #IranWar #Geopolitics #NewWorldOrder #WakeUp #June2026

BLESS YOUR POLITICS 

Trump has declared war on wind and solar — sources of energy that, as far as anyone can tell, have never colluded with a foreign government or caused a geopolitical crisis. Meanwhile, oil — which has done both, repeatedly — remains the centerpiece of his “energy dominance” vision. The irony is thick enough to drill through: the one commodity most vulnerable to international price shocks, supply manipulations, and Middle Eastern instability is the one he’s betting the country on. Renewables don’t fluctuate with OPEC decisions. They don’t spike when a tanker gets seized in the Strait of Hormuz. They don’t give you asthma. But sure — let’s cancel all that and go back to being held hostage by a barrel price we’ve never controlled and never will.
Given the skyrocketing price oil, how does he fail to see the value of renewables is a mystery. Apparently, stupid is as stupid does

America First Energy Policy
We are the world’s #1 oil producer.
American families should not be paying $5+ a gallon because of Iran, OPEC, or refinery global profit chasing.

Simple rule: During clear national emergencies — wars, hostile supply manipulation, or prices spiking above $4/gallon (inflation-adjusted) — refined oil products are prioritized for the domestic market first. Only true excess gets exported.
This isn’t price fixing. It’s national security.
Iran and our enemies couldn’t economically wait us out if high oil prices didn’t hammer American wallets. Trump (or any president) could play hardball as long as needed without our own people bleeding at the pump.
Oil companies would still make solid profits. They just wouldn’t get record windfall profits every time the world goes to shit. Maybe we’d even start fewer wars if Big Oil wasn’t incentivized to love chaos.
Our oil. Our land. Our people first.
No more putting global markets and donor profits ahead of American families.
Who else is tired of this?
